title: I burned all my tokens researching how to save tokens
excerpt: How I burned a full Claude limit in 30 minutes and built my own deep research pipeline instead: 3 subscriptions, shared memory, a clear role for each model. You can build the same from what you already pay for.
content_html:
On the Hacker News front page on 19 Jul 2026

At Quesma we are researching the economics of AI agents: what agentic coding really costs and what you can do about it. For this research I am running my own deep research setup, a pipeline of agents that builds a knowledge base I can actually trust. The first version of this setup burned the whole limit of my Claude Max 5x plan in 30 minutes. This post is the story of how I fixed the cost and the trust, using only subscriptions I already pay for, and how you can build the same.

My goal was to understand the entire state of so-called tokenomics. I wanted to know what monitoring systems exist, how teams govern their AI spend, and which optimization tools and practices actually work, both in the papers and in the real world.

I started the usual way, with /deep-research. I gave it the big open question and let it run. After around 30 minutes of research, I hit a limit and had to wait a few hours for it to reset. And I had no results. The run launched 111 agents and queued 123 claims for verification, but only 25 got verified before the limit hit, and the final synthesis never ran.

So, it hit me personally. And this is a bit funny: from the very first day I had to optimize tokens while still discovering how to optimize tokens. Learning by doing.

Using every subscription I already pay for

If Claude Fable 5 is out after 30 minutes and /deep-research is consuming so many tokens without giving me any results, what can I do to make the research more effective? I started thinking about what kind of tools I already have and pay for. Claude, Codex, and Antigravity: 3 subscriptions, and in theory 3 times more tokens without paying anything extra. What if I use all of these tools together, with shared memory?

Since I am already using the claude-mem plugin I extended it to support Codex and Antigravity locally, so that all 3 tools can use shared memory during sessions. Whatever one tool learns, the others can use.

Cheaper models as subagents

My default setup is Claude Code, so I used it as the main harness. While doing the research manually, I discovered a model-orchestration pattern, which was exactly what I needed at that moment. We don’t really need Fable for everything: Claude Opus 4.8, Claude Sonnet 5, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro are already excellent models for many tasks.

So I checked a few benchmarks and cost analyses, mostly Terminal-Bench for terminal and agentic work, SWE-bench Pro for end-to-end software engineering, and Artificial Analysis for a general view of performance and prices. I did not treat any of them as the final truth; benchmarks measure their own thing and the numbers move every month. I just needed a loose starting point for who is good at what, and using several different models was part of the experiment from the beginning; I expected to adjust the split after real runs anyway:

Role Model Why this one
Find Claude Sonnet 5 Strong on agentic benchmarks and cheap enough to run in bulk
Verify Claude Opus 4.8 The most accurate Claude worker; checking needs more accuracy than searching
Judge & plan Claude Fable 5 The most expensive one, so it only plans, decomposes, and resolves disputes
Small stuff Claude Haiku 4.5 Cheap and fast for extraction and formatting, too weak for multi-step work
Run tools Codex (GPT-5.5) Very strong on terminal benchmarks; clones, installs, runs, and inspects tools
Second opinion Antigravity (Gemini 3.1 Pro) A different model family, so it does not share the same blind spots

This split was not my first version; I adjusted it a few times when live runs showed weaknesses, and the fallback rules came from those failures.

The best thing here is that I prepared Codex and Antigravity to be used as Claude subagents, orchestrated by Fable, using the headless nature of both. Why is it cool? Because tokens are taken from the Codex and Antigravity subscriptions, so I was not paying extra, but I instantly had much more intelligence to use.

The whole trick is one small Bash script that my Claude agents can call like any other command:

# run-cli: call another vendor's CLI as a headless subagent
# usage: run-cli <codex|antigravity> "<prompt>"
VENDOR="$1"; PROMPT="$2"
case "$VENDOR" in
  codex)       OUT="$(codex exec --sandbox read-only "$PROMPT")" ;;
  antigravity) OUT="$(agy --model "Gemini 3.1 Pro (High)" -p "$PROMPT")" ;;
esac
echo "$OUT"
echo "$OUT" | claude-mem-save -s "$VENDOR" # save to shared memory (my locally extended claude-mem)

The wrapper also watches the output for “usage limit” and “out of credits” messages and returns a special exit code, and this is exactly how the automatic fallback to Claude models works: the orchestrator sees the signal and does the work with a Claude agent instead.

One more thing that really matters is to pin the model per role, because subagents inherit their parent’s model by default, and that is exactly how I burned my Fable limit in 30 minutes.

With this technique I was able to run the research continuously for roughly 10 times longer than initially with Fable only, measured simply as how long I can keep the agents working before any of the 3 subscriptions hits a limit. Before it was 30 minutes of research, now it is a few hours, without paying a single penny more. And when Codex or Antigravity hits its own limit, the framework simply falls back to Claude models, so the research does not stop.

Reducing hallucinations

Cost was only the first problem, the second one was trust. Before my research framework, when everything was done by Fable, I was sometimes getting findings that looked solid but were not true. For example, a wrong license on a repo, a savings figure with no source, or a number that was not on the cited page. To reduce hallucinations, I implemented explicit rules in the research framework that every finding must pass before it can be shared. A few of them:

  • Whoever finds a claim never verifies it, a different model or agent checks the links, quotes, and numbers.
  • Nothing lands in the knowledge base without a URL and a quote from the primary source.
  • Never state a number the source page doesn’t contain.

This list was not designed upfront. It grew during the research, because every time verification caught a new class of mistake, the rule went back into the prompts. And such a framework is really effective only if you keep tuning it, so review the findings, flag the useless ones, and feed that back.

/deep-research goes last

With cost and trust handled, the last piece was the /deep-research tool that started this whole story. It is still in the pipeline, but as the last step, not the first. At the end of each day I run it over the findings that already survived verification, so instead of researching blindly it goes through the existing findings, deepens them, eliminates the mess, and tries to fill the gaps that the framework missed. It also uses fewer tokens this way, because it works through a fixed list of claims instead of exploring the internet. The last run used 61 agents and took 22 minutes. Compare that to day one, when 111 agents burned the whole limit in around 30 minutes and never produced the report. Same tool, just a much smaller job.

The result: a knowledge base I can trust

Everything that survives verification lands in an LLM wiki I keep in Obsidian, inspired by Karpathy’s LLM wiki pattern: linked atomic notes, agents doing the sweeps, and me setting the rules. One week in, it holds hundreds of validated notes on pricing, tools, benchmarks, and practices.

Automated checks aren’t enough on their own. Once, my triage rule quietly rejected Headroom, a 56k-star project and one of the biggest in its category. The agents did everything right, verification confirmed the project is legit, and a claim check correctly flagged that its headline savings numbers were not quality-controlled. But my rule said “unvalidated claims mean no entry”, so a project half the industry uses was invisible in my knowledge base. I only noticed 2 days later, when I asked “how did we miss this?” The fix was a new rule: reject the claim, not the project. Agents can do the searching and checking, but no agent will ever tell you that your own rule is the bug.

If you want to build a quality knowledge base, human verification and research supplementation are mandatory. AI-only research can be really poor quality. Human-only research is too slow. The best way is a hybrid one: agents do the heavy research work, but they run on a pipeline crafted and constantly improved by a human. The human also verifies the results and fills the gaps.

A few findings from the knowledge base

The token economics topic turned out to be much larger than I expected. Here is a small sample of what is already inside, the findings that surprised me the most:

  1. Your harness can matter as much as your model. On Terminal-Bench the same model shows a ~66x token spread across different harnesses, and the thinner setups actually score higher, not lower. Another study measured a swing of about 54 percentage points on the same model, changing only the harness. We saw a small version of this ourselves when we checked the true cost of saying “Hi” to an AI agent.
  2. Context compaction can double your bill instead of saving it. Compaction sounds like compression, so it is easy to assume it is always good, but it is not free: the model has to summarize your history and that summarization call consumes tokens too. It can also evict files the agent still needs, so the agent re-reads them, the context fills up again, and compaction fires again. One documented regression shows how bad this loop can get: after a harness retuned its compaction threshold, it went from 4 to 12-26 compactions per session, and token usage on identical tasks went from 89 million to 160-185 million.
  3. One mid-session tool change silently re-bills everything. Cache reads cost about 0.1x of the base input price, but the cache invalidates as a hierarchy (tools, then system, then messages). Add or reorder a single tool schema in the middle of a session and the entire cached prefix re-bills at full price. You get no error, just a bigger invoice.
  4. Your token counter is not your invoice. In one documented case a calculated $3.60/month landed as a $25-40/month invoice, a 7-11x gap coming from context accumulation, retry amplification, framework overhead, and evaluation calls that a simple token estimate never captured.

Our framework tags most of these as medium confidence, usually 1 or 2 solid sources, and we keep that visible instead of pretending it is settled.

Before you burn your next limit

If you are burning your limits on deep research today, try reversing the order. Cheap models find, accurate models verify, and deep research goes last. And check how much intelligence you already pay for. A few subscriptions with a clear role for each model give you much more than one frontier model used blindly.

And if you are building your own research pipeline, or your AI bill grows faster than your usage, we would really like to hear from you. Join the discussion on Hacker News or LinkedIn.

Stay tuned for future posts and releases

blocks.html_en: One more thing that really matters is to pin the model per role, because subagents inherit their parent’s model by default, and that is exactly how I burned my Fable limit in 30 minutes.

blocks.html_en: With this technique I was able to run the research continuously for roughly 10 times longer than initially with Fable only, measured simply as how long I can keep the agents working before any of the 3 subscriptions hits a limit. blocks.html_en: Reducing hallucinations

sentences.en: Reducing hallucinations

blocks.html_en: Cost was only the first problem, the second one was trust. Before my research framework, when everything was done by Fable, I was sometimes getting findings that looked solid but were not true. For example, a wrong license on a repo, a savings figure with no source, or a number that was not on the cited page. To reduce hallucinations, I implemented explicit rules in the research framework that every finding must pass before it can be shared. A few of them:

sentences.en: Cost was only the first problem, the second one was trust.
sentences.zh: 成本只是第一个问题，第二个问题是信任。

sentences.en: Before my research framework, when everything was done by Fable, I was sometimes getting findings that looked solid but were not true.
sentences.zh: 在我的研究框架之前，当一切都是由《寓言》完成时，我有时会得到看起来可靠但并不真实的发现。

sentences.en: For example, a wrong license on a repo, a savings figure with no source, or a number that was not on the cited page.
sentences.zh: 例如，存储库上的许可证错误、没有来源的储蓄数字或引用页面上没有的数字。

sentences.en: To reduce hallucinations, I implemented explicit rules in the research framework that every finding must pass before it can be shared.
sentences.zh: 为了减少幻觉，我在研究框架中实施了明确的规则，即每项发现都必须通过才能共享。

sentences.en: A few of them:
sentences.zh: 其中一些：

blocks.html_en: • Whoever finds a claim never verifies it, a different model or agent checks the links, quotes, and numbers.

sentences.en: Whoever finds a claim never verifies it, a different model or agent checks the links, quotes, and numbers.
sentences.zh: 无论谁发现索赔，都不会对其进行验证，而是由不同的模型或代理检查链接、报价和数字。

blocks.html_en: • Nothing lands in the knowledge base without a URL and a quote from the primary source.

sentences.en: Nothing lands in the knowledge base without a URL and a quote from the primary source.
sentences.zh: 如果没有 URL 和主要来源的引用，任何内容都不会进入知识库。

blocks.html_en: • Never state a number the source page doesn’t contain.

sentences.en: Never state a number the source page doesn’t contain.
sentences.zh: 切勿注明源页面不包含的数字。

blocks.html_en: This list was not designed upfront. It grew during the research, because every time verification caught a new class of mistake, the rule went back into the prompts. And such a framework is really effective only if you keep tuning it, so review the findings, flag the useless ones, and feed that back.

sentences.en: This list was not designed upfront.
sentences.zh: 该列表不是预先设计的。

sentences.en: It grew during the research, because every time verification caught a new class of mistake, the rule went back into the prompts.
sentences.zh: 它在研究过程中不断发展，因为每次验证发现一类新的错误时，该规则都会回到提示中。

sentences.en: And such a framework is really effective only if you keep tuning it, so review the findings, flag the useless ones, and feed that back.
sentences.zh: 只有不断调整，这样的框架才真正有效，因此请检查调查结果，标记无用的框架，然后反馈。

blocks.html_en: With cost and trust handled, the last piece was the /deep-research tool that started this whole story. It is still in the pipeline, but as the last step, not the first. At the end of each day I run it over the findings that already survived verification, so instead of researching blindly it goes through the existing findings, deepens them, eliminates the mess, and tries to fill the gaps that the framework missed. It also uses fewer tokens this way, because it works through a fixed list of claims instead of exploring the internet. The last run used 61 agents and took 22 minutes. Compare that to day one, when 111 agents burned the whole limit in around 30 minutes and never produced the report. Same tool, just a much smaller job.

blocks.html_en: Everything that survives verification lands in an LLM wiki I keep in Obsidian, inspired by Karpathy’s LLM wiki pattern: linked atomic notes, agents doing the sweeps, and me setting the rules. One week in, it holds hundreds of validated notes on pricing, tools, benchmarks, and practices.

blocks.html_en: Hundreds of notes on pricing, tools, and benchmarks so far.

blocks.html_en: Automated checks aren’t enough on their own. Once, my triage rule quietly rejected Headroom, a 56k-star project and one of the biggest in its category. The agents did everything right, verification confirmed the project is legit, and a claim check correctly flagged that its headline savings numbers were not quality-controlled. But my rule said “unvalidated claims mean no entry”, so a project half the industry uses was invisible in my knowledge base. I only noticed 2 days later, when I asked “how did we miss this?” The fix was a new rule: reject the claim, not the project. Agents can do the searching and checking, but no agent will ever tell you that your own rule is the bug.

sentences.en: Automated checks aren’t enough on their own.
sentences.zh: 自动化检查本身还不够。

sentences.en: Once, my triage rule quietly rejected Headroom, a 56k-star project and one of the biggest in its category.
sentences.zh: 有一次，我的分类规则悄悄地拒绝了 Headroom，这是一个 56k 星级项目，也是同类项目中最大的项目之一。

sentences.en: The agents did everything right, verification confirmed the project is legit, and a claim check correctly flagged that its headline savings numbers were not quality-controlled.
sentences.zh: 代理商所做的一切都是正确的，验证证实了该项目是合法的，索赔检查正确地表明其总体储蓄数字没有受到质量控制。

sentences.en: But my rule said “unvalidated claims mean no entry”, so a project half the industry uses was invisible in my knowledge base.
sentences.zh: 但我的规则是“未经验证的声明意味着禁止进入”，因此行业一半使用的项目在我的知识库中是不可见的。

sentences.en: I only noticed 2 days later, when I asked “how did we miss this?”
sentences.zh: 两天后，当我问“我们怎么会错过这个？”时，我才注意到这一点。

sentences.en: The fix was a new rule: reject the claim, not the project.
sentences.zh: 解决办法是一条新规则：拒绝索赔，而不是项目。

sentences.en: Agents can do the searching and checking, but no agent will ever tell you that your own rule is the bug.
sentences.zh: 代理可以进行搜索和检查，但没有代理会告诉您您自己的规则是错误。

blocks.html_en: If you want to build a quality knowledge base, human verification and research supplementation are mandatory. AI-only research can be really poor quality. Human-only research is too slow. The best way is a hybrid one: agents do the heavy research work, but they run on a pipeline crafted and constantly improved by a human. The human also verifies the results and fills the gaps.

sentences.en: If you want to build a quality knowledge base, human verification and research supplementation are mandatory.
sentences.zh: 如果你想建立一个高质量的知识库，人工验证和研究补充是必须的。

sentences.en: AI-only research can be really poor quality.
sentences.zh: 纯人工智能研究的质量可能真的很差。

sentences.en: Human-only research is too slow.
sentences.zh: 仅针对人类的研究太慢了。

sentences.en: The best way is a hybrid one: agents do the heavy research work, but they run on a pipeline crafted and constantly improved by a human.
sentences.zh: 最好的方法是混合方法：代理承担繁重的研究工作，但它们运行在由人类精心设计和不断改进的管道上。

sentences.en: The human also verifies the results and fills the gaps.
sentences.zh: 人类还验证结果并填补空白。

blocks.html_en: A few findings from the knowledge base

sentences.en: A few findings from the knowledge base
sentences.zh: 知识库中的一些发现

blocks.html_en: The token economics topic turned out to be much larger than I expected. Here is a small sample of what is already inside, the findings that surprised me the most:

sentences.en: The token economics topic turned out to be much larger than I expected.
sentences.zh: 事实证明，代币经济学话题比我预期的要大得多。

sentences.en: Here is a small sample of what is already inside, the findings that surprised me the most:
sentences.zh: 以下是内部已有内容的一小部分样本，其中最令我惊讶的发现是：

blocks.html_en: • Your harness can matter as much as your model. On Terminal-Bench the same model shows a ~66x token spread across different harnesses, and the thinner setups actually score higher, not lower. Another study measured a swing of about 54 percentage points on the same model, changing only the harness. We saw a small version of this ourselves when we checked the true cost of saying “Hi” to an AI agent.

sentences.en: Your harness can matter as much as your model.
sentences.zh: 您的安全带与您的型号一样重要。

sentences.en: On Terminal-Bench the same model shows a ~66x token spread across different harnesses, and the thinner setups actually score higher, not lower.
sentences.zh: 在 Terminal-Bench 上，同一模型显示不同线束之间的令牌分布约为 66 倍，而且较薄的设置实际上得分更高，而不是更低。

sentences.en: Another study measured a swing of about 54 percentage points on the same model, changing only the harness.
sentences.zh: 另一项研究测量了同一型号的摆动约 54 个百分点，仅改变了安全带。

sentences.en: We saw a small version of this ourselves when we checked the true cost of saying “Hi” to an AI agent.
sentences.zh: 当我们检查向人工智能代理说“嗨”的真实成本时，我们自己看到了这个的一个小版本。

blocks.html_en: • Context compaction can double your bill instead of saving it. Compaction sounds like compression, so it is easy to assume it is always good, but it is not free: the model has to summarize your history and that summarization call consumes tokens too. It can also evict files the agent still needs, so the agent re-reads them, the context fills up again, and compaction fires again. One documented regression shows how bad this loop can get: after a harness retuned its compaction threshold, it went from 4 to 12-26 compactions per session, and token usage on identical tasks went from 89 million to 160-185 million.

sentences.en: Context compaction can double your bill instead of saving it.
sentences.zh: 上下文压缩可以使您的账单增加一倍，而不是节省费用。

sentences.en: Compaction sounds like compression, so it is easy to assume it is always good, but it is not free: the model has to summarize your history and that summarization call consumes tokens too.
sentences.zh: 压缩听起来像压缩，因此很容易假设它总是好的，但它不是免费的：模型必须总结您的历史记录，并且汇总调用也会消耗令牌。

sentences.en: It can also evict files the agent still needs, so the agent re-reads them, the context fills up again, and compaction fires again.
sentences.zh: 它还可以逐出代理仍然需要的文件，以便代理重新读取它们，上下文再次填充，并再次触发压缩。

sentences.en: One documented regression shows how bad this loop can get: after a harness retuned its compaction threshold, it went from 4 to 12-26 compactions per session, and token usage on identical tasks went from 89 million to 160-185 million.
sentences.zh: 一份记录在案的回归显示了这个循环的糟糕程度：在线束重新调整其压缩阈值后，每个会话的压缩次数从 4 次增加到 12-26 次，相同任务上的令牌使用量从 8900 万增加到 160-18500 万。

blocks.html_en: • One mid-session tool change silently re-bills everything. Cache reads cost about 0.1x of the base input price, but the cache invalidates as a hierarchy (tools, then system, then messages). Add or reorder a single tool schema in the middle of a session and the entire cached prefix re-bills at full price. You get no error, just a bigger invoice.

sentences.en: One mid-session tool change silently re-bills everything.

content_html_zh:
2026 年 7 月 19 日黑客新闻头版

在 Quesma，我们正在研究人工智能代理的经济学：代理编码的真正成本是多少以及你可以采取什么措施。 对于这项研究，我正在运行自己的深度研究设置，这是一个代理管道，可以构建我真正可以信任的知识库。 此设置的第一个版本在 30 分钟内就耗尽了我的 Claude Max 5x 计划的全部限制。 这篇文章讲述了我如何仅使用我已经付费的订阅来确定成本和信任，以及如何构建相同的内容。

我的目标是了解所谓的代币经济学的整个状态。 我想知道存在哪些监控系统，团队如何管理他们的人工智能支出，以及哪些优化工具和实践在论文和现实世界中实际有效。

我以通常的方式开始，使用/deep-research。 我给了它一个悬而未决的大问题并让它运行。 经过大约 30 分钟的研究后，我达到了极限，不得不等待几个小时才能重置。 而我却没有任何结果。 该运行启动了 111 个代理，并对 123 个声明进行了排队等待验证，但在达到限制之前只有 25 个得到验证，并且最终的合成从未运行。

所以，这对我个人来说是打击。 这有点有趣：从第一天起，我就必须优化令牌，同时仍在探索如何优化令牌。 边做边学。

使用我已经付费的每个订阅

如果《克劳德寓言 5》在 30 分钟后发布，而 /deep-research 消耗了如此多的代币却没有给我任何结果，我该怎么做才能使研究更有效？ 我开始思考我已经拥有并付费的工具类型。 Claude、Codex 和 Antigravity：3 次订阅，理论上无需支付任何额外费用即可获得 3 倍的代币。 如果我将所有这些工具一起使用并共享内存会怎么样？

由于我已经在使用 claude-mem 插件，因此我将其扩展为本地支持 Codex 和 Antigravity，以便所有 3 个工具都可以在会话期间使用共享内存。 无论一种工具学到了什么，其他工具都可以使用。

更便宜的模型作为子代理

我的默认设置是 Claude Code，所以我用它作为主要工具。 在手动进行研究时，我发现了一种模型编排模式，这正是我当时所需要的。 我们并不真的需要 Fable 来解决所有问题：Claude Opus 4.8、Claude Sonnet 5、GPT-5.5 和 Gemini 3.1 Pro 已经是许多任务的优秀模型。

因此，我检查了一些基准测试和成本分析，主要是用于终端和代理工作的 Terminal-Bench，用于端到端软件工程的 SWE-bench Pro，以及用于性能和价格总体视图的 Artificial Analysis。 我没有把其中任何一个当作最终的真理；基准衡量自己的事物，并且数字每个月都会变化。 我只需要一个宽松的起点来确定谁擅长什么，并且使用几种不同的模型从一开始就是实验的一部分；无论如何，我希望在实际运行后调整分割：

Role Model 为什么是这个
Find 克劳德十四行诗 5 具有强大的代理基准，并且足够便宜，可以批量运行
核实 近距离工作 4.8 最精准的克劳德工；检查比搜索需要更高的准确性
判断与计划 克劳德寓言5 最昂贵的，所以它只是计划、分解、解决纠纷
小东西 克劳德俳句 4.5 提取和格式化便宜且快速，但对于多步骤工作来说太弱
运行工具 法典 (GPT-5.5) 终端基准测试非常强大；克隆、安装、运行和检查工具
第二意见 反重力（Gemini 3.1 Pro） 不同的型号系列，因此不存在相同的盲点

这种分裂并不是我的第一个版本；当实时运行显示出弱点时，我对其进行了几次调整，并且后备规则来自于这些失败。

这里最好的事情是，我准备了 Codex 和 Antigravity 作为 Claude 的子代理，由 Fable 精心策划，利用两者的无头性质。 为什么很酷？ 因为代币是从 Codex 和 Antigravity 订阅中获取的，所以我没有支付额外费用，但我立即有了更多的情报可以使用。

整个技巧是一个小的 Bash 脚本，我的 Claude 代理可以像任何其他命令一样调用它：

# run-cli: call another vendor's CLI as a headless subagent
# usage: run-cli <codex|antigravity> "<prompt>"
VENDOR="$1"; PROMPT="$2"
case "$VENDOR" in
  codex)       OUT="$(codex exec --sandbox read-only "$PROMPT")" ;;
  antigravity) OUT="$(agy --model "Gemini 3.1 Pro (High)" -p "$PROMPT")" ;;
esac
echo "$OUT"
echo "$OUT" | claude-mem-save -s "$VENDOR" # save to shared memory (my locally extended claude-mem)

包装器还监视“使用限制”和“积分不足”消息的输出，并返回一个特殊的退出代码，这正是自动回退到 Claude 模型的工作原理：编排器看到信号并与 Claude 代理一起完成工作。

真正重要的另一件事是固定每个角色的模型，因为子代理默认继承其父级的模型，而这正是我在 30 分钟内烧毁《神鬼寓言》限制的方式。

通过这项技术，我能够连续运行研究的时间比最初仅使用《神鬼寓言》的时间长大约 10 倍，简单地衡量为在 3 个订阅中的任何一个达到限制之前我可以让代理工作多长时间。 以前需要30分钟的研究，现在只需几个小时，而且无需多付一分钱。 当 Codex 或 Antigravity 达到其自身极限时，框架就会退回到 Claude 模型，因此研究不会停止。

减少幻觉

成本只是第一个问题，第二个问题是信任。 在我的研究框架之前，当一切都是由《寓言》完成时，我有时会得到看起来可靠但并不真实的发现。 例如，存储库上的许可证错误、没有来源的储蓄数字或引用页面上没有的数字。 为了减少幻觉，我在研究框架中实施了明确的规则，即每项发现都必须通过才能共享。 其中一些：

  • 无论谁发现索赔，都不会对其进行验证，而是由不同的模型或代理检查链接、报价和数字。
  • 如果没有 URL 和主要来源的引用，任何内容都不会进入知识库。
  • 切勿注明源页面不包含的数字。

该列表不是预先设计的。 它在研究过程中不断发展，因为每次验证发现一类新的错误时，该规则都会回到提示中。 只有不断调整，这样的框架才真正有效，因此请检查调查结果，标记无用的框架，然后反馈。

/深入研究最后

处理好成本和信任后，最后一部分是开始整个故事的/深度研究工具。 它仍在酝酿中，但作为最后一步，而不是第一步。 每天结束时，我都会对已经通过验证的发现进行检查，因此它不会盲目研究，而是会仔细检查现有的发现，深化它们，消除混乱，并尝试填补框架遗漏的空白。 通过这种方式，它使用的代币也更少，因为它通过固定的声明列表来工作，而不是探索互联网。 上次运行使用了 61 个代理，耗时 22 分钟。 与第一天相比，当时 111 名特工在大约 30 分钟内耗尽了整个限制，但从未生成报告。 相同的工具，只是工作量小得多。

结果：我可以信赖的知识库

所有通过验证的内容都会出现在我保留在 Obsidian 中的 LLM wiki 中，其灵感来自于 Karpathy 的 LLM wiki 模式：链接的原子注释、代理进行扫描以及我设置规则。 一周后，它就保存了数百份有关定价、工具、基准和实践的经过验证的笔记。

自动化检查本身还不够。 有一次，我的分类规则悄悄地拒绝了 Headroom，这是一个 56k 星级项目，也是同类项目中最大的项目之一。 代理商所做的一切都是正确的，验证证实了该项目是合法的，索赔检查正确地表明其总体储蓄数字没有受到质量控制。 但我的规则是“未经验证的声明意味着禁止进入”，因此行业一半使用的项目在我的知识库中是不可见的。 两天后，当我问“我们怎么会错过这个？”时，我才注意到这一点。 解决办法是一条新规则：拒绝索赔，而不是项目。 代理可以进行搜索和检查，但没有代理会告诉您您自己的规则是错误。

如果你想建立一个高质量的知识库，人工验证和研究补充是必须的。 纯人工智能研究的质量可能真的很差。 仅针对人类的研究太慢了。 最好的方法是混合方法：代理承担繁重的研究工作，但它们运行在由人类精心设计和不断改进的管道上。 人类还验证结果并填补空白。

知识库中的一些发现

事实证明，代币经济学话题比我预期的要大得多。 以下是内部已有内容的一小部分样本，其中最令我惊讶的发现是：

  1. 您的安全带与您的型号一样重要。 在 Terminal-Bench 上，同一模型显示不同线束之间的令牌分布约为 66 倍，而且较薄的设置实际上得分更高，而不是更低。 另一项研究测量了同一型号的摆动约 54 个百分点，仅改变了安全带。 当我们检查向人工智能代理说“嗨”的真实成本时，我们自己看到了这个的一个小版本。
  2. 上下文压缩可以使您的账单增加一倍，而不是节省费用。 压缩听起来像压缩，因此很容易假设它总是好的，但它不是免费的：模型必须总结您的历史记录，并且汇总调用也会消耗令牌。 它还可以逐出代理仍然需要的文件，以便代理重新读取它们，上下文再次填充，并再次触发压缩。 一份记录在案的回归显示了这个循环的糟糕程度：在线束重新调整其压缩阈值后，每个会话的压缩次数从 4 次增加到 12-26 次，相同任务上的令牌使用量从 8900 万增加到 160-18500 万。
  3. 会话中的一项工具更改会默默地重新计费所有内容。 缓存读取成本约为基本输入价格的 0.1 倍，但缓存作为层次结构（工具、系统、消息）失效。 在会话中间添加或重新排序单个工具模式，整个缓存的前缀将按全价重新计费。 你不会得到任何错误，只是一张更大的发票。
  4. 您的令牌计数器不是您的发票。 在一个有记录的案例中，计算出的 3.60 美元/月的发票金额为 25-40 美元/月，由于上下文积累、重试放大、框架开销和评估调用而产生了 7-11 倍的差距，而简单的令牌估计从未捕获过。

我们的框架将其中大部分标记为中等可信度，通常是 1 或 2 个可靠来源，并且我们保持可见而不是假装它已解决。

在你燃烧下一个极限之前

如果您今天正在突破深入研究的极限，请尝试颠倒顺序。 廉价的模型发现，精确的模型验证，深入研究最后。 并检查您已经支付了多少情报费用。 每种模型都有明确作用的一些订阅为您提供的不仅仅是一种盲目使用的前沿模型。

如果您正在构建自己的研究渠道，或者您的人工智能账单增长速度快于您的使用量，我们非常希望收到您的来信。 加入 Hacker News 或 LinkedIn 上的讨论。

请继续关注未来的帖子和版本
